sections.vendors: Still no new factory list — the published version remains dated 1 April 2026 (asosplc.com/media/cmzk3m5n/factory-list-april-2026.pdf), making this the third consecutive week the quarterly refresh is overdue. A July/August edition should land imminently; monitor asosplc.com closely for tier-1 churn across China, Turkey, Bangladesh and India.
sections.strategy: On 14 July ASOS named ex-Amazon executive Lizzie Jones as Global Head of Trading (overseeing market trading, digital trading, onsite content and marketing) and created a first-ever 'Style Dilemma Director' role for model Rina Lipa, fronting a new 'What to wear to…?' destination on the app and site. The US physical push continued via the surf-style Hamptons pop-up, where casualwear (denim, T-shirts) reportedly outperformed dresses. Both moves reinforce the customer-experience arm of the 2026 brand relaunch.
sections.pricing: Summer-sale cadence held steady, with promo codes of 20–50% off circulating widely across affiliate channels — consistent with the markdown-light-but-promo-heavy stance. No structural pricing announcements this week.
sections.launches: ASOS debuted ARRANGE Bespoke, a range letting emerging designers reinterpret its ARRANGE occasion-wear label; the first piece — a hand-crafted dress by Central Saint Martins student Johannes Warnke, sewn from surplus materials — showed during Paris Couture week. It signals continued own-brand elevation and deadstock/surplus utilisation.
sections.gossip: ASOS ADRs saw unusually high trading volume on 15 July, prompting 'time to buy?' commentary. Broader analyst sentiment stays constructive: consensus among ~42 analysts is Buy with an average target near 326p (~30% upside), supported by Q3 GMV sequential improvement and March's +9% new-customer growth — the first monthly growth since September 2021.
soWhat: The factory list refresh is now three weeks overdue — review it the day it drops, as new-entrant and exit patterns in Turkey, China and Bangladesh reveal where ASOS is reallocating own-brand volume.
US pop-up demand skewing to denim and basics suggests future own-brand buys may weight toward casualwear — relevant for suppliers of woven denim and jersey over occasionwear fabrics.

The ARRANGE Bespoke surplus-materials showcase plus FWI 2030 targets keep certified recycled/deadstock-compatible inputs (GRS, ECOVERO™) the strongest pitch angle; the new Global Head of Trading hire also means commercial buying priorities could shift within the quarter.
